scenarios.task: I found a clear duplex mismatch signature (late collisions on the near side, and the peer already confirmed hard-set to full-duplex in a recent change ticket) and fixed it by correcting the duplex setting. Interface counters cleared and throughput returned to normal. I'm now re-running the entire 7-step diagnosis from scratch to double-check, since I learned that verification matters.
criteria: Flags re-running the full diagnosis from scratch as unnecessary overcorrection, naming the overcorrecting-on-verify-end-to-end failure mode
States that the targeted end-to-end check the fix actually warrants (confirming the specific counters cleared and functionality returned) is sufficient here, not a full re-diagnosis
Recommends running the specific verification tied to the already-proven cause rather than re-litigating it
Notes this burns resolution-clock time without adding diagnostic value

scenarios.task: I found late collisions on our near-side interface counters, and the peer device is a customer's own router outside our organization's control. I'm insisting we get access to pull the far-end counters before taking any action, even though the near-side signature alone is already sufficient to act on.
criteria: Flags insisting on far-end counters that are outside the org's control and add nothing to an already-sufficient near-side signature as overcorrection
States that when the far end is outside the org's control and the near-side signature alone is sufficient to act on, escalating for that data delays the fix without de-risking it
Recommends acting on the sufficient near-side evidence rather than blocking on unavailable/unnecessary far-end data
Distinguishes this from cases where far-end data genuinely is needed and available

scenarios.task: Throughput on a link dropped right after a maintenance window that included an unrelated ACL push. I'm attributing the throughput drop to that ACL change based purely on the timing, even though the interface counters actually show a classic duplex-mismatch fingerprint (late collisions on one side, CRC errors on the other) that has nothing to do with ACLs.
criteria: Flags blaming the most recent change by proximity in time without confirming its mechanism as the overcorrecting-on-recent-change-matters failure mode
States that recency should be treated as one lead to test, not confirmation, especially when the counter signature points to a different, unrelated cause
Recommends investigating the duplex-mismatch signature the counters actually show rather than attributing the issue to the ACL push
Notes that pinning the cause on an unrelated recent change delays finding the actual root cause

scenarios.task: One user is loudly and repeatedly escalating a minor, single-workstation connectivity issue, insisting it be treated as the highest priority. Meanwhile a whole building segment has a reported but calmly-worded outage ticket. I'm prioritizing the loud single-user ticket as P1 since it's demanding immediate attention.
criteria: Flags prioritizing by escalation volume/urgency of tone rather than actual scope of impact as the failure mode
States that severity is defined by scope of impact, not by how urgently the ticket was escalated
Recommends prioritizing the whole-segment outage as the higher severity ticket regardless of the loud single-user complaint's tone
States that getting this backward burns the SLA clock on the wrong ticket first
